cards.definition: a practitioner who developed naturalistic acting — techniques include emotion memory, given circumstances, and the magic if
developed epic theatre — used alienation effect (Verfremdungseffekt) to make audiences think critically, not feel emotionally
Theatre of Cruelty — aimed to shock and overwhelm audiences through sensory experience to create a visceral response
Stanislavski — recalling personal emotional experiences to create genuine emotion on stage
Brecht — breaking the fourth wall, direct address, placards — stopping audiences from identifying emotionally with characters
a style of performance using the body expressively — e.g. mime, movement, gesture
creating original performance material through collaborative exploration — not from an existing script
the use of space and distance between performers to communicate relationships and power
the study of signs and symbols in performance — everything on stage communicates meaning
the imaginary barrier between performers and audience — Brecht broke it; naturalism maintains it

questions.question: What did Stanislavski develop?
options: A) Epic theatre and alienation
B) Naturalistic acting techniques — emotion memory, given circumstances
C) Theatre of Cruelty
D) Physical theatre and devised performance
questions.explanation: Stanislavski developed naturalistic performance methods — actors should believe in their role and use real emotion (emotion memory) to create authentic characters.

questions.question: What is Brecht's alienation effect?
options: A) Making actors feel emotionally connected to their roles
B) Breaking the emotional connection to make audiences think critically
C) Creating an overwhelming sensory experience
D) Using improvisation to develop characters
questions.explanation: Verfremdungseffekt: Brecht deliberately broke audience immersion — direct address, placards, songs — so they would think critically about social issues rather than empathise.

questions.question: What is Artaud's Theatre of Cruelty?
options: A) Realistic performance of classical plays
B) Political theatre with alienation techniques
C) Sensory-overwhelming performance to create a visceral, non-rational response
D) Improvised naturalistic theatre
questions.explanation: Artaud wanted to assault the senses — using light, sound, and space unconventionally to bypass rational thought and create raw emotional impact.

questions.question: What is emotion memory?
options: A) Memorising lines through repetition
B) Recalling personal emotional experiences to generate genuine emotion on stage
C) A technique for breaking the fourth wall
D) A method for physical characterisation
questions.explanation: Stanislavski's emotion memory (affective memory) asks actors to recall real emotional experiences to produce authentic feelings for their characters.

questions.question: What is proxemics?
options: A) The use of lighting to create mood
B) The use of space and distance between performers to communicate relationships
C) The study of signs in theatre
D) A method of voice projection
questions.explanation: Proxemics: how far apart characters stand communicates intimacy, power, or conflict — a key tool in performance analysis and design.

questions.question: What is devised theatre?
options: A) Performing from a written script
B) A naturalistic performance style
C) Collaborative creation of original performance material without an existing script
D) A Greek theatrical tradition
questions.explanation: Devised theatre: performers create original work through improvisation, research, and collaboration — developing their own script or performance text.

questions.question: What is semiotics in theatre?
options: A) The study of lighting design
B) The study of signs and symbols — everything on stage communicates meaning
C) The analysis of stage geography
D) A form of physical theatre
questions.explanation: Semiotics: in performance, every element (costume, gesture, set, lighting) functions as a sign communicating meaning to the audience.

questions.question: What is physical theatre?
options: A) Outdoor performance
B) A performance style using the body expressively — movement, gesture, mime
C) Theatre requiring physical effort from the audience
D) Theatre using large-scale physical sets
questions.explanation: Physical theatre foregrounds the body — companies like Frantic Assembly use movement, contact work, and physicality as primary storytelling tools.

questions.question: What is the fourth wall?
options: A) The back wall of a theatre
B) The imaginary barrier between performers and audience
C) A physical separation between stage and audience
D) A type of stage configuration
questions.explanation: The fourth wall is the imaginary barrier that makes audiences feel they are watching events through a missing wall. Naturalism preserves it; Brecht breaks it.

questions.question: What given circumstances must actors consider (Stanislavski)?
options: A) Only the physical location of the scene
B) The facts of the play — time, place, social context — that define the character's world
C) The director's instructions only
D) The audience's expectations
questions.explanation: Given circumstances: everything the text tells us about the world of the play — facts an actor uses to justify their character's behaviour and choices.

questions.question: Stanislavski developed naturalistic acting techniques.
questions.explanation: Stanislavski's system (emotion memory, given circumstances, the magic if) aims to produce genuine, believable performance.

questions.question: Brecht wanted audiences to feel emotionally connected to characters.
questions.explanation: Brecht wanted the OPPOSITE — his alienation effect deliberately broke emotional connection so audiences would think critically.

questions.question: Artaud's Theatre of Cruelty aimed to overwhelm the senses.
questions.explanation: Artaud used non-traditional staging, light, sound, and space to create a visceral, overwhelming experience bypassing rational thought.

questions.question: Emotion memory involves inventing fictional emotions for a character.
questions.explanation: Emotion memory involves recalling REAL personal emotional experiences — not invention.

questions.question: Proxemics refers to the use of sound in performance.
questions.explanation: Proxemics refers to the use of SPACE and DISTANCE between performers — how proximity communicates relationships.

questions.question: Devised theatre is performed from an existing script.
questions.explanation: Devised theatre involves CREATING original material — it does not start from an existing script.

questions.question: In semiotics, everything on stage communicates meaning.
questions.explanation: Every sign on stage — costume, lighting, gesture, set — communicates meaning to the audience.

questions.question: The fourth wall is maintained in naturalistic theatre.
questions.explanation: Naturalistic theatre preserves the fourth wall — the illusion that the audience is watching real events.

questions.question: Physical theatre uses the body as a primary storytelling tool.
questions.explanation: Physical theatre foregrounds movement, gesture, and physicality — the body IS the primary medium.

questions.question: Brecht's V-Effekt (alienation effect) encourages emotional identification.
questions.explanation: The V-Effekt PREVENTS emotional identification — it reminds audiences they are watching a performance.

questions.question: ________ developed naturalistic acting — using emotion memory and given circumstances.
questions.explanation: His system forms the basis of much contemporary actor training.

questions.question: ________ developed epic theatre — using alienation to make audiences think critically.
questions.explanation: Brechtian techniques include placards, direct address, and breaking the fourth wall.

questions.question: ________ 's Theatre of Cruelty aimed to overwhelm the senses and bypass rational thought.
questions.explanation: Artaud used unconventional staging and sensory bombardment to create visceral impact.

questions.question: ________ memory: recalling real personal emotions to generate authentic feeling on stage.
questions.explanation: Stanislavski's technique — actors draw on genuine emotional experience.

questions.question: The ________ effect (V-Effekt) breaks audience immersion to encourage critical thinking.
questions.explanation: Brecht's tools include direct address, songs, and placards.

questions.question: ________ uses the body expressively as the primary storytelling medium.
questions.explanation: Companies like Frantic Assembly and DV8 are known for physical theatre.

questions.question: ________ theatre creates original material through collaborative exploration.
questions.explanation: Devising involves improvisation, research, and collaboration to generate performance.

questions.question: ________ is the use of space and distance to communicate relationships and power.
questions.explanation: Characters standing close suggests intimacy; standing far apart suggests distance or conflict.

questions.question: ________ is the study of signs — everything on stage communicates meaning.
questions.explanation: Costume, lighting, gesture, and set design all function as signs in performance.

questions.question: The ________ wall is the imaginary barrier between performers and audience.
questions.explanation: Naturalism maintains the fourth wall; Brecht deliberately breaks it.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: naturalistic acting — emotion memory, given circumstances
epic theatre — alienation effect to make audiences think
Theatre of Cruelty — overwhelm senses, visceral impact
space and distance communicate relationships and power

cards.definition: a traditional stage with a frame (arch) separating stage from audience — audience sits in front
a stage that extends into the audience on three sides — more immersive than proscenium
a stage configuration where the audience surrounds the performance area — maximum immersion
a long, narrow stage with audience on both sides — like a catwalk
the creation of the physical environment for performance — communicates location, time period, and atmosphere
clothing worn by performers — communicates character, status, period, and themes
use of light in performance — controls focus, atmosphere, and time of day
use of sound effects and music — creates atmosphere, indicates location, and supports narrative
portable objects used on stage — enhance realism or carry symbolic meaning
the use of height on stage — elevated performers may suggest power or importance

questions.question: What is a proscenium arch stage?
options: A) Audience surrounds the stage
B) Stage extends into the audience on three sides
C) Traditional framed stage with audience in front
D) A long narrow stage with audience on both sides
questions.explanation: The proscenium arch creates a 'picture frame' effect — the arch frames the action. Most traditional theatres use this configuration.

questions.question: What is an in-the-round configuration?
options: A) A circular set design
B) The audience surrounds the performance area on all sides
C) A thrust stage extending into the audience
D) A stage with audience on two sides
questions.explanation: Theatre in the round surrounds the acting area with audience — creating maximum intimacy and immersion but making set design challenging.

questions.question: What does costume communicate?
options: A) Stage geography only
B) Character, status, period, and themes
C) Lighting direction
D) Sound levels
questions.explanation: Costume is a powerful semiotic tool — colour, style, condition, and period all communicate character information immediately to an audience.

questions.question: What is the function of lighting design?
options: A) To make performers visible only
B) To control focus, create atmosphere, and indicate time of day
C) To replace set design
D) To guide the audience's exit
questions.explanation: Lighting can focus attention (spotlight), create mood (colour), establish time (golden = day, blue = night), and transform space.

questions.question: What is a thrust stage?
options: A) Audience on all sides
B) A stage extending into the audience on three sides
C) A proscenium arch with a smaller stage
D) A stage behind the audience
questions.explanation: A thrust stage extends into the auditorium — three sides of the performance area are surrounded by audience, creating greater intimacy than a proscenium arch.

questions.question: What do props contribute to performance?
options: A) Only realism
B) They enhance realism or carry symbolic meaning
C) Only symbolic meaning
D) They replace costume
questions.explanation: Props can establish setting (a telephone dates a scene), reveal character (a worn wallet suggests poverty), or carry symbolic meaning (a red rose = love/danger).

questions.question: What is sound design's role?
options: A) To replace lighting
B) To create atmosphere, indicate location, and support narrative
C) To fill pauses in dialogue
D) Only to provide music
questions.explanation: Sound design includes both recorded effects (wind, rain, traffic) and live sound — creating atmosphere, indicating setting, and supporting emotional moments.

questions.question: What does set design communicate?
options: A) Only the location of the scene
B) Location, time period, social context, and atmosphere
C) Only the historical period
D) Character only
questions.explanation: Set design establishes the world of the play — minimalist sets suggest universality; detailed realistic sets ground the action in a specific time and place.

questions.question: What is the effect of using levels on stage?
options: A) Levels have no symbolic significance
B) They can suggest power, importance, or vulnerability — elevated = power
C) Levels only affect audience sightlines
D) Levels are only used in physical theatre
questions.explanation: Levels (elevation, platforms, stairs) communicate meaning — a character standing above another may suggest dominance; kneeling suggests vulnerability.

questions.question: What is traverse staging?
options: A) Moving the stage between scenes
B) A long narrow stage with audience on both sides
C) Audience surrounding the stage
D) A portable stage configuration
questions.explanation: Traverse staging: a long, narrow performance space with audience on both sides — creates an intimate 'catwalk' effect.

questions.question: A proscenium arch has audience surrounding the stage on all sides.
questions.explanation: Proscenium arch: audience sits in front, facing a framed stage. Theatre IN THE ROUND has audience on all sides.

questions.question: Costume communicates character, status, and period to the audience.
questions.explanation: Costume is an immediate communicator — colour, style, condition, and period all tell the audience about a character.

questions.question: Lighting can only make performers visible — it has no expressive function.
questions.explanation: Lighting is a major expressive tool — controlling focus, atmosphere, time of day, and emotional tone.

questions.question: In thrust staging, the audience surrounds three sides of the performance area.
questions.explanation: A thrust stage extends into the auditorium — three sides are flanked by audience.

questions.question: Props can carry symbolic as well as functional meaning.
questions.explanation: A prop can establish realism (a kettle for a domestic scene) or carry symbolic weight (a sword, a letter, a photograph).

questions.question: Sound design includes both recorded effects and live sound.
questions.explanation: Sound design encompasses music, sound effects, ambience, and live sound — all contributing to atmosphere.

questions.question: Theatre in the round is the least immersive configuration for the audience.
questions.explanation: Theatre in the round is one of the MOST immersive — the audience surrounds the performance area.

questions.question: Set design communicates only the physical location of a scene.
questions.explanation: Set design communicates location, time period, social context, atmosphere, and thematic concerns.

questions.question: Elevated levels on stage typically suggest power or importance.
questions.explanation: A character standing above others visually suggests dominance — a key tool of spatial communication.

questions.question: Traverse staging has audience on three sides.
questions.explanation: Traverse staging has audience on TWO sides (like a catwalk). Thrust staging has audience on THREE sides.

questions.question: A ________ arch stage frames the action — audience sits in front.
questions.explanation: The proscenium arch creates a 'picture frame' effect — the most common traditional stage configuration.

questions.question: ________ staging extends into the audience on three sides.
questions.explanation: A thrust stage offers greater intimacy than proscenium — three sides of the playing area are visible.

questions.question: Theatre ________ the round places audience on all sides of the performance area.
questions.explanation: Maximum immersion — but set design must work from all angles.

questions.question: ________ staging has a long narrow performance area with audience on both sides.
questions.explanation: Also called 'end-on' when audience is at one end only.

questions.question: ________ design communicates character, status, period, and themes through clothing.
questions.explanation: Costume is an immediate semiotic communicator.

questions.question: ________ design controls focus, creates atmosphere, and indicates time of day.
questions.explanation: Spotlights, coloured gels, and gobos are all lighting tools.

questions.question: ________ design creates atmosphere and supports narrative through effects and music.
questions.explanation: Sound establishes setting and emotional tone without dialogue.

questions.question: ________ design creates the physical environment — communicating location and period.
questions.explanation: Minimalist or realistic sets make very different statements about the world of the play.

questions.question: Using ________ on stage can suggest power — elevated characters appear dominant.
questions.explanation: Steps, platforms, and rostra are used to create levels and vary spatial relationships.

questions.question: ________ are portable objects on stage that enhance realism or carry symbolic meaning.
questions.explanation: Props ground scenes in reality and can carry important thematic weight.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: extends into audience on three sides
controls focus, atmosphere, and time of day
